excerpt: Chartlog is $14.99/mo with strong visual chart review but no free tier. Full breakdown of every plan, hidden costs, and who should pay for it in 2026.
content:
Chartlog Pricing Overview 2026

If you're a visual trader who lives and dies by chart patterns, Chartlog has built something genuinely useful: a trading journal that automatically captures chart screenshots and overlays your trade data directly on those charts. But before you commit, you need to know exactly what you're paying for — and what you're giving up compared to alternatives.

Chartlog's headline price is $14.99/month, which puts it in the mid-range of trading journal software. It earns a solid 3.9/5 rating from traders, largely on the strength of its visual review workflow. This guide breaks down every plan, what's included, where the value is, and whether it's the right fit for your trading style in 2026.

Chartlog Plans and Costs

Chartlog keeps its pricing structure relatively simple compared to competitors that layer on three or four tiers. Here's what's currently available:

  • Lite Plan: Entry-level access with core journaling features, automatic chart screenshot capture, and basic trade tagging. This tier is where Chartlog delivers its best value proposition — the screenshot automation alone saves day traders significant time during post-session review.
  • Pro Plan (~$14.99/mo): The full-featured tier with trade data overlay on charts, expanded analytics, and deeper filtering. This is the published price point most traders encounter. Annual billing typically reduces this, though Chartlog's specific annual discount should be confirmed on their pricing page before purchasing.

One important note upfront: Chartlog does not offer a free tier. This is a meaningful distinction from competitors like Kinfo, which has a free entry point. If you want to test Chartlog before committing financially, look for trial periods — but budget for a paid subscription from day one.

What You Get for $14.99 Per Month

The core value of Chartlog is its visual review system. Most trading journals force you to manually upload charts or work entirely with numbers. Chartlog automates the screenshot process, then layers your actual entry, exit, stop loss, and target data directly onto the captured chart image.

For a chart pattern trader or day trader reviewing 20-50 trades per week, this changes the review workflow significantly. Instead of cross-referencing a spreadsheet with saved chart images, you're looking at a single unified view: the chart as it appeared, with your trade plotted on it.

Key features included at the paid tier:

  • Auto chart screenshots — captures the chart at trade entry and exit without manual action
  • Trade data overlay — entry price, exit price, P&L, and notes rendered on the chart image
  • Trade tagging and filtering — categorize by setup type, session, instrument
  • Performance metrics — win rate, average R, streak data, and equity curve
  • Pattern library — build a personal reference of working setups with visual examples

What's notably absent at this price point: Chartlog lacks the deep statistical analysis engine found in premium competitors. If you want Monte Carlo simulations, multi-dimensional playbook analysis, or the kind of granular psychological tagging that tools like Edgewonk offer, you'll hit Chartlog's ceiling quickly.

Hidden Fees and Costs to Watch in 2026

The published $14.99/mo is largely what you pay — Chartlog doesn't have an aggressive upsell structure. However, there are practical costs to factor in:

  • No free tier means no zero-cost testing. You're committing real money before you know if the workflow fits. Factor in at least one month ($14.99) as an evaluation cost.
  • Annual vs. monthly billing: Like most SaaS tools, annual billing typically saves 15-25%. If you're committing long-term, verify the annual price directly — it can meaningfully change the cost-per-month calculation.
  • Integration dependencies: Chartlog works best when connected to your broker for automated trade imports. If your broker isn't supported, you're manually entering trades — which negates much of the time-saving value proposition.
  • Storage considerations: Automatic chart screenshots accumulate. Confirm whether there are storage caps at your plan level that could affect how far back your visual archive goes.

Compared to some competitors that charge separately for additional users or limit the number of trades per month at lower tiers, Chartlog's pricing model is relatively transparent. The main "hidden cost" is really the opportunity cost of its analytical limitations if you eventually outgrow it.

Chartlog vs. Alternatives: Quick Comparison

Chartlog occupies a specific niche in the trading journal market. Here's how it stacks up against the tools traders most commonly consider alongside it:

ToolPriceRatingFree TierBest ForStandout Feature
Chartlog$14.99/mo3.9/5NoVisual/chart pattern tradersAuto chart screenshots + overlay
Edgewonk~$169/yr one-time4.4/5NoAnalytical traders, serious journalingDeep statistical playbook analysis
JournalytixFree–$49/mo4.1/5YesActive day tradersReal-time trade streaming + analytics
KinfoFree–$29/mo3.8/5YesSocial/community tradersPublic trade sharing + leaderboards

For a deeper look at how these tools compare head-to-head, see the Chartlog vs. Edgewonk comparison, the Chartlog vs. Journalytix breakdown, and the Chartlog vs. Kinfo analysis. Each covers specific use cases where one tool has a clear edge over the other.

Who Should Pay for Chartlog

Chartlog's pricing makes sense for a specific trader profile. You'll get strong value from this tool if:

  • You trade chart patterns repeatedly. The visual archive of your trades becomes a pattern library over time. Seeing 50 examples of your "bull flag entry" alongside their outcomes is genuinely instructive.
  • You're a day trader doing post-session review. The automatic screenshot capture means your review workflow happens in minutes, not the 30-45 minutes it takes to manually reconstruct your trade context.
  • You're in the $14.99/mo budget range. Chartlog's Lite tier in particular delivers solid visual journaling without the full price of more analytically complex tools.
  • You don't need advanced statistics. If you're not yet running simulations or calculating expectancy across 500+ trade samples, Chartlog's feature set covers your current needs.

Chartlog is a harder sell if you're primarily a systems trader who needs deep quantitative analysis, if your broker isn't supported for automated imports, or if you're looking to start for free before committing to a paid plan.

Chartlog Pricing Verdict 2026

At $14.99/month, Chartlog is priced fairly for what it delivers to its target audience. The automatic chart screenshot feature is genuinely differentiated — no other tool at this price point makes visual trade review as frictionless. For chart pattern traders and visual learners, that workflow improvement has real compounding value over months of use.

The 3.9/5 rating reflects a tool that does its core thing well but has meaningful gaps for traders who eventually want to go deeper analytically. It's not a tool you'll grow into — it's a tool you might grow out of.

Our recommendation: If visual trade review is your primary journaling goal and you trade chart-based setups, Chartlog at the Lite tier is good value. Budget $14.99 for a trial month, connect your broker, and run one full week of post-session reviews. If the screenshot overlay workflow clicks for you, the annual plan is worth committing to. If you find yourself wishing for more statistical depth within that first month, the full Chartlog review lays out the specific analytical gaps — and points to alternatives built for that use case.

The no-free-tier policy is the biggest friction point in 2026, especially when competitors like Kinfo let you start for nothing. But for the right trader, the paid commitment is justified by a genuinely useful visual review system that saves real time every trading day.
